coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of a fluorescent cation surfactant derived from carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Ping
Kuai, Yong
Wu, Qing
Zheng, Yi
Liu, Xiang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with easier preparation, lower manufacture cost and fluorescent feature caught extensive interests. Herein, a technique of synthesizing a novel cationic surfactant, derived from CQDs via chemical modification, was reported in this work in detail. Firstly, the CQDs were synthesized by aqueous solutions of citric acid and 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine under hydrothermal condition at 180 °C for 60 min. Then the CQDs reacted with excess 1-chlorododecane in alcohol medium via quaterisation for synthesizing the CQDs cationic surfactant. During the processes, the solvent alcohol as well as the excess of 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine and 1-chlorododecane could be isolated efficiently and recycled. The experimental results showed that the CQDs cationic surfactant possessed versatility such as a good performance of reducing the surface tension of water, wonderful antibacterial effect on colibacillus at a lower concentration and a high fluorescent quantum yield.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Chemical modification
Surfactant
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CQDs and the successive quaterisation with 1-chlorohexadecane
Fluorescence, surfactivity and antibacterial effect of CQDs-C12H25

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the CQDs; (b) IR spectra of the raw materials and the CQDs; (c) Fluorescent and ultraviolet-visible spectra of the CQDs under varied excitation wavelengths; (d) Regressed lines between the integrated fluorescent intensity and the absorbance of quinine sulfate and the CQDs.
1H NMR of the CQDs and CQDs-C12H25 corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively.
(a) Fluorescent spectra of CQDs-C12H25; (b) Surface tension of water at varied concentration of CQDs-C12H25; (c) Antibacterial rates at varied concentration of CQDs-C12H25.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of a fluorescent cation surfactant derived from carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jinquan Lubricants Technology Co. Ltd, Maanshan 243131, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with easier preparation, lower manufacture cost and fluorescent feature caught extensive interests. Herein, a technique of synthesizing a novel cationic surfactant, derived from CQDs via chemical modification, was reported in this work in detail. Firstly, the CQDs were synthesized by aqueous solutions of citric acid and 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine under hydrothermal condition at 180 °C for 60 min. Then the CQDs reacted with excess 1-chlorododecane in alcohol medium via quaterisation for synthesizing the CQDs cationic surfactant. During the processes, the solvent alcohol as well as the excess of 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine and 1-chlorododecane could be isolated efficiently and recycled. The experimental results showed that the CQDs cationic surfactant possessed versatility such as a good performance of reducing the surface tension of water, wonderful antibacterial effect on colibacillus at a lower concentration and a high fluorescent quantum yield.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or nano dots with convenient preparation, lower expenditure and fluorescent feature evoked extensive interests on their manufactures and applications. Usually, the raw materials of preparing CQDs were small molecules with multiply functional groups such as carboxyl [1], hydroxyl [2], amino groups [3], etc. Their crosslink under intensive conditions resulted in the production of CQDs with well hydrophily. Until today there were numerous reports about the preparing techniques and fluorescent applications in biological domains [3,4] on account of the lower toxicity [5]. The works about the chemical modification on CQDs and the successive application were rare [6]. In fact, the inherent oxygen- or nitrogen-contained groups endued hydrophily of the CQDs. If the CQDs were modified by a hydrophobic group, they should possess the dual property of hydrophily and hydrophobicity that served as a surfactant. Above all, the hydrophily was derived from the CQDs themselves rather than from additional chemical synthesizing.

Herein we designed an approach of manufacturing the CQDs containing tertiary amino groups. Successively they were quaternized by halogenated n-dodecane. Depending on the hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc and hydrophobic group of the long carbon chain such CQDs cationic surfactant should have a good performance in surfactivity. This type of cationic surfactant derived from CQDs was scarcely to be reported until today.

Citric acid monohydrate (99.8%), 3-(dimethylamino)-1-propylamine (DMAPA, 99%), 1-chlorododecane (97%) and quinine sulfate dehydrate (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were used as received. Double distilled water was used throughout the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CQDs and the successive quaterisation with 1-chlorohexadecane
ce:section.ce:para: Weighed 3.00 g citric acid (0.014 mol) monohydrate and dissolved completely in 5 mL water. The solution was transferred to a Teflon vessel and mixed with 6 mL DMAPA (0.048 mol). The pH value of the mixture was 9.5. The excess DMAPA were more easily to be isolated from the product than citric acid due to the lower boiling point of DMAPA. Moreover, that ensures the enough tertiary amino groups in the CQDs. Such hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 60 min. At last, the solution was transferred to a round flask which was distilled with reduced pressure at 30 °C for removing excess DMAPA which was reused in the next preparation of the CQDs. The remaining was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 60 min for obtaining the CQDs. Such CQDs were mixed with 14 mL absolute alcohol. The mixtures were disposed ultrasonically until the CQDs were dissolved completely. The 6 mL 1-chlorohexadecane (0.025 mol) were added in and the quaterisation reaction was executed with a reflux heating at 70 °C for 5 h. Amount of 10 mL water were put in with a violent shake after the alcohol was completely removed. The aqueous layer was collected and dried in an oven at 70 °C for obtaining the cationic surfactant denoted with CQDs-C12H25. The excess 1-chlorohexadecane in oil layer was recycled.

The CQDs were witnessed by image of transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN). The quaterisation reaction was demonstrated by the comparison of 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR, BRUKER 400, TMS as an internal standard) spectra of the CQDs and CQDs-C12H25. The ultraviolet-visible and fluorescent spectra of CQDs and CQDs-C12H25 were measured for estimating the fluorescent quantum yield [7]. Surface tension of the aqueous solution of CQDs-C12H25 was estimated at 25 °C with maximum bubble pressure method.

Antibacterial rate of CQDs-C12H25 on colibacillus was measured with a 96-well plate. Firstly, took a 0.5 mL centrifuge tube and added 90 µL LB medium as well as 10 µL CQDs-C12H25 solution (100.0 mg mL−1). The mixture was shaken completely on a micro oscillator. Thus the concentration of CQDs-C12H25 was 10.0 mg mL−1. Then measured 50 µL of the mixture and added in 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes with 450 µL test bacteria solutions. The concentration of CQDs-C12H25 was 1.0 mg mL−1 now.

Repeated the same procedure and obtained various concentrations of CQDs-C12H25 in test bacteria solution. Successively, added the solutions in a 96-wells plate (including a sample of positive control). Fetched 3 × 100 µL from the solution and added in three parallel holes. The 96-wells plate was placed in a biochemical incubator with a constant temperature of 37 °C for an incubation of 18 h. The OD (optical density) values were measured with an enzymatic marker and the antibacterial rate were calculated according to these OD values.

CQDs with the lower preparing cost and wonderful fluorescent feature should have more extensive applications. The dense functional groups on their surface provide a convenience of chemical modification. In this work, DMAPA is chosen intentionally as a raw material for preparing the CQDs. Thus the tertiary amino groups are introduced into the produced CQDs that provide a convenience of realizing the quaterisation with halogenated alkane. The produced CQDs containing tertiary amino groups are illustrated by Fig. 1 (a). The crystalline planes are discerned clearly in the inset of Fig. 1.

IR spectra of Fig. 2 (b) reveal the structural changes. The stretching vibration of CO in citric acid disappears completely, which predicts the combination of carboxyl and primary amino groups of DMAPA and entire consumption of citric acid. The resulted amide is demonstrated by the absorption peaks at 1652 cm−1 (amide I). The wide absorption near 3500 cm−1 should be assigned to overlapping of hydroxyl and amino groups. These functional groups guarantee the hydrophilicity of the CQDs. The ultraviolet-visible and fluorescent spectra are revealed in Fig. 2(c). Ultraviolet-visible spectrum shows the characteristic absorbing of the CQDs locates near 340 nm. And the fluorescent spectra disclose the unvaried emission at 450 nm under the varied excitations. This predicts the homogeneous particle sizes. Integrated fluorescence intensities (excited wavelength = 340 nm) of the CQDs with varied concentrations and the corresponding absorbance are depicted in Fig. 2(d). The fluorescent quantum yield of the CQDs can be obtained by a comparison with standard substance of quinine sulfate in solution of 0.5 M H2SO4 [8]. The value reaches 23.49% according to the slope of the regressed lines in Fig. 2(d).

The CQDs can be quaternized by 1-chlorohexadecane. Chemical shifts of 1H in Fig. 2(a) suggest the CQDs should be composed of –NH 2 (1.02–1.06 ppm), –CH 2–CHCH2– (1.65–1.68 ppm), –CH2CH 2–NH2 (2.24–2.51 ppm), –CH2–CH 2–(CO)– (2.73–2.77 ppm), –CH2–CH 2–OCH2– (3.40–3.45 ppm) and –CH2–CH CH–CH2– (5.56–6.52 ppm). Compared with Fig. 2(a), the obvious variation of chemical shifts in Fig. 2(b) are marked with red stars, which should be arisen from –CH 3 (0.83–0.86 ppm) and –CH2CH 2CH2– (1.17–1.31 ppm). These predict the accomplishment of quaterisation between the CQDs and 1-chlorohexadecane. The long carbon chain (–C12H25) endow hydrophobicity of the CQDs-C12H25. Due to the inherent dense hydrophilic groups of the CQDs, the CQDs-C12H25 should behave surface activity on water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence, surfactivity and antibacterial effect of CQDs-C12H25
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical modification on CQDs-C12H25 with 1-chlorohexadecane does not change the fluorescent emission, which illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). The fluorescent peaks remain at 450 nm approximately under excitation of varied wavelengths. The fluorescent quantum yield is 3.54%, which exclaims maintaining of the fluorescence.

Fig. 3(b) shows the surface tension of water drops more than a half and reaches 32 mN m−1 when the concentration of CQDs-C12H25 arrives at 0.7 mg mL−1 (critical micelle concentration). This proves the surfactivity of CQDs-C12H25. The wonderful property of reducing the surface tension of water is superior to most of the reported gemini cationic surfactant [9–11]. The surface hydrophilic groups of the CQDs and the attached long carbon chain which acts as hydrophobic group donate the surfactivity of CQDs-C12H25. Compared with conventional technique of synthesizing surfactant such as sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate, the procedure of manufacturing CQDs-C12H25 is more facile. Moreover, the fluorescent emission of CQDs-C12H25 provides the capability of fluorescent whitening, which applied extensively in mixed detergent.

Fig. 3(c) demonstrates the well antibacterial rate on colibacillus. When the concentration of CQDs-C12H25 is greater than 0.5 mg mL−1, the antibacterial rate surpasses 90%. This is similar with some conventional [12,13] and special surfactants [14,15]. Thus such a new type of cationic surfactant integrates the multiple functions in one.

The CQDs containing tertiary amino groups are manufactured conveniently. Their inherent oxygen-contained functional groups provide hydrophily of the novel cationic surfactant of CQDs-C12H25. The excess of DMAPA and 1-chlorohexadecane can be isolated and recycled during the manufacture of CQDs-C12H25. Therefore, we report a facile and efficient technique of synthesizing CQDs cationic surfactant with versatility of fluorescence, surfactivity and antibacterial activities. The approach extends the application of CQDs in more areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge very helpful discussions with Dr. Zi Liu and her assistances during the biological experiments.
